EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across northwest Maine to the Gulf of Saint Lawrence during the 1st...drawing a cold front across the region producing damaging winds. The high winds across northern and eastern Maine occurred in several phases. In the first phase...onshore southerly winds with a very strong low level jet affected Downeast areas during the morning. The most significant impacts with this phase generally occurred along the Downeast coast. Wind gusts along the coast generally ranged from 55 to 65 mph...with isolated gusts up to around 70 mph. The second phase occurred with winds associated with a convective line of showers along the cold front which also crossed the region during the morning. Wind gusts with the line of showers generally ranged from 40 to 50 mph. These winds affected much of the region. The third phase of the event occurred with west-southwest gradient winds which developed during the afternoon in the wake of the cold front and persisted into the late afternoon and evening. Wind gusts with this phase of the event generally ranged from 50 to 60 mph...with the greater impacts across northern and central portions of the region.|Power outages rapidly increased during the 1st...peaking at around 62000 customers across northern and eastern Maine. Power was not completely restored in some areas until later on the 3rd. Numerous trees were snapped or toppled along with broken branches. Utility poles were also toppled or damaged in many areas. The fallen trees and utility poles blocked many roads...leading to road closures and slowing the recovery process. Heavy rains occurred with the system leading to wet soils. The wet soil and changing wind directions likely contributed to the large number of toppled trees. Damage to the roof and siding of an elementary school was reported in Surry...along the Hancock county coast. A stained glass window was blown out of a church in Caribou...in northeast Aroostook county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked northeast across the state during the 24th...with a strong low level jet moving along the Downeast coast during the afternoon and evening. South to southwest winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 65 mph...along the Downeast coast. The winds toppled trees which contributed to power outages mostly across portions of Hancock county where power outages peaked at around 6600 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South to southwest winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of mostly 50 to 60 mph. However...a peak gust of 63 mph was measured at the Bar Harbor Airport. The winds toppled trees which contributed to around 6600 customers losing power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure lifted toward Maine during the 29th...reaching southern Maine during the early morning hours of the 30th. The low then lifted north across Maine through the 30th. Snow developed from the late evening of the 29th into the early morning hours of the 30th. The heaviest snows generally occurred to the west of the low track. To the east of the track...warmer air was drawn north allowing the snow to transition to a wintry mix and rain. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late morning and early afternoon of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred through the morning. Warning criteria snow accumulations also occurred through the morning. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to around 12 inches across northwest Aroostook county. Gusty winds produced areas of blowing snow across northern areas during the afternoon. South of the track...across Downeast areas...precipitation transitioned to a wintry mix and rain. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight on the 8th a warm front lifted into the Northeast and stalled along the coast. A strong upper level vorticity maximum crossed the the stalled boundary on the morning of the 9th and led to rapid cyclogenesis along the New England coastline. Low pressure moved northeast along the Maine coast before moving into New Brunswick. While it was cold enough to snow in the mountains, warmer temperatures in the low to mid levels allowed for precipitation to begin as a mix of sleet, freezing rain, and rain to the south. As low pressure strengthened, heights aloft lowered and precipitation transitioned to heavy snow as it exited the area. Low level moisture and northwest flow behind the departing system kept upslope snow showers going into the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light mixed precipitation changed over to moderate to heavy snow during the morning of the 9th. Snow was especially heavy in the higher terrain of the Mahoosucs, with lighter snow farther south through the foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked along the Downeast coast to eastern Maine from the night of the 8th through the 9th. Light overrunning snows developed during the 8th...with heavier snow arriving through the night of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th. Deformation zone snows then persisted through the afternoon and early evening of the 9th...diminishing late evening. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred at times during the afternoon of the 9th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 13 inches. Gusty winds in the wake of the storm produced areas of blowing snow across northern areas during the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 12 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light mixed precipitation changed over to moderate snow during the morning of the 9th. A persistent north-south band of snowfall into the evening allowed accumulating snow to continue much later in the day than areas farther west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight on the 8th a warm front lifted into the Northeast and stalled along the coast. A strong upper level vorticity maximum crossed the the stalled boundary on the morning of the 9th and led to rapid cyclogenesis along the New England coastline. Low pressure moved northeast along the Maine coast before moving into New Brunswick. While it was cold enough to snow in the mountains, warmer temperatures in the low to mid levels allowed for precipitation to begin as a mix of sleet, freezing rain, and rain to the south. As low pressure strengthened, heights aloft lowered and precipitation transitioned to heavy snow as it exited the area. Low level moisture and northwest flow behind the departing system kept upslope snow showers going into the morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snowfall developed on the morning of the 9th and pivoted over the mountains of western Maine. Snow accumulations reached 6 inches or more by midday. Low pressure moving into New Brunswick kept winds northwest over the area into the overnight, and low level moisture forced over the mountains resulted in additional upslope snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid, Canadian high pressure nosed into the Northeast on the 19th and settled there into the first part of the 20th. Low pressure developed in the southern Plains and lifted through the lower Mississippi and then Ohio Valley. Strong warm air advection along the warm front, pinned offshore by the strong high pressure, allowed secondary cyclogensis to occur farther up the Northeast coast as the open wave aloft approached. Heavy snow overspread western Maine early in the morning on the 20th. As warm air advection continued ahead of the upper level wave precipitation changed over predominantly sleet, as far north as the mountains of western Maine, before ending as snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow moved into the western Maine mountains in the early morning hours and continued through the afternoon. Mixed precipitation stayed to the south, and snowfall totals ranged from 12 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying coastal low approached from the southwest during the 20th...tracked along the Downeast coast that night...exiting across the Maritimes during the 21st. Snow expanded across the region during the early morning hours of the 20th. Snow intensities rapidly increased through the remainder of the morning with heavy snow developing. Heavy snow then persisted through the afternoon into the evening...with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times in the heavier snow bands. Winds also steadily increased through the 20th with occasional gusts of 30 to 40 mph producing near blizzard conditions at times. Snow diminished...from south to north...through the evening of the 20th into the early morning hours of the 21st. Lighter snows persisted across northern areas into the morning of the 21st before ending. However...extensive blowing and drifting snow persisted across northern areas through the remainder of the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd. Storm total snow accumulations across northern portions of Aroostook...Somerset and Piscataquis counties generally ranged from 18 to 22 inches with local totals to around 24 inches. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late morning into early afternoon of the 20th.|South and east of a line from central Piscataquis...to northern Penobscot...to southeast Aroostook counties the snow mixed with or changed to sleet. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 18 inches in an area from Houlton...to Millinocket...to Greenville along with 1 to 3 inches of sleet. Snow accumulations across the remainder of the region...inland  from the coast...generally ranged from 8 to 14 inches along with 1 to 4 inches of sleet. Along the Downeast coast...snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 13 inches along with 1 to 4 inches of sleet and a tenth to quarter inch of ice. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late morning into early afternoon of the 20th.|A high astronomical tide...along with large waves with the major coastal storm...contributed to minor coastal flooding along the Downeast coast around the time of high tide on the morning of the 20th. Minor coastal flooding was reported on several roads and locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 15 to 20 inches...with local totals up to 25 inches. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph. A record daily snowfall of 16.9 inches occurred at Caribou on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta clipper crossed the northern tier of the country on the 28th and arrived in the eastern Great Lakes by the 29th. As a jet streak rounded the base of the upper trough secondary low pressure developed off the New England coast. Low pressure spread snow along and north of its track as it moved towards Downeast Maine. Snow was moderate to heavy at times in the morning before quickly ending from west to east. Warmer temperatures near the coast did allow some of the snow to change to sleet, freezing rain, and rain for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent light snow along a weak warm front developed on the evening of the 29th over parts of the western Maine mountains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid, Canadian high pressure nosed into the Northeast on the 19th and settled there into the first part of the 20th. Low pressure developed in the southern Plains and lifted through the lower Mississippi and then Ohio Valley. Strong warm air advection along the warm front, pinned offshore by the strong high pressure, allowed secondary cyclogensis to occur farther up the Northeast coast as the open wave aloft approached. Heavy snow overspread western Maine early in the morning on the 20th. As warm air advection continued ahead of the upper level wave precipitation changed over predominantly sleet, as far north as the mountains of western Maine, before ending as snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of light to moderate snow developed over parts of western Maine late in the evening of the 19th in response to warm air advection developing aloft. Several inches of snow fell before heavy snow moved into area in the early morning hours. Snow changed to significant amounts of sleet during the morning and continued for much of the day. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta clipper crossed the northern tier of the country on the 28th and arrived in the eastern Great Lakes by the 29th. As a jet streak rounded the base of the upper trough secondary low pressure developed off the New England coast. Low pressure spread snow along and north of its track as it moved towards Downeast Maine. Snow was moderate to heavy at times in the morning before quickly ending from west to east. Warmer temperatures near the coast did allow some of the snow to change to sleet, freezing rain, and rain for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent light snow along a weak warm front developed on the evening of the 29th over parts of southwestern Maine. Secondary low pressure developed in the Gulf of Maine overnight and snowfall intensified through the early morning hours on the 30th. Snow quickly ended from west to east in the morning, with occasional mixed precipitation. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid, Canadian high pressure nosed into the Northeast on the 19th and settled there into the first part of the 20th. Low pressure developed in the southern Plains and lifted through the lower Mississippi and then Ohio Valley. Strong warm air advection along the warm front, pinned offshore by the strong high pressure, allowed secondary cyclogensis to occur farther up the Northeast coast as the open wave aloft approached. Heavy snow overspread western Maine early in the morning on the 20th. As warm air advection continued ahead of the upper level wave precipitation changed over predominantly sleet, as far north as the mountains of western Maine, before ending as snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow broke out across the southwestern Maine coast just after midnight on the 20th, with heavy snow moving into area in the early morning hours. Snow changed to significant amounts of sleet during the morning and continued for much of the day, keeping snow amounts lower than areas farther north. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 9 inches. The highest totals were observed just inland from the coast, where ocean effect enhancement and coastal front convergence likely contributed to greater low level lift.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of light to moderate snow developed over parts of western Maine late in the evening on the 19th in response to warm air advection aloft. Then heavy snow moved into area in the early morning hours. Snow changed to significant amounts of sleet during the morning and continued for much of the day, keeping snow amounts lower than areas farther north. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked along the Downeast coast to eastern Maine from the night of the 8th through the 9th. Light overrunning snows developed during the 8th...with heavier snow arriving through the night of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th. Deformation zone snows then persisted through the afternoon and early evening of the 9th...diminishing late evening. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred at times during the afternoon of the 9th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 13 inches. Gusty winds in the wake of the storm produced areas of blowing snow across northern areas during the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta clipper crossed the northern tier of the country on the 28th and arrived in the eastern Great Lakes by the 29th. As a jet streak rounded the base of the upper trough secondary low pressure developed off the New England coast. Low pressure spread snow along and north of its track as it moved towards Downeast Maine. Snow was moderate to heavy at times in the morning before quickly ending from west to east. Warmer temperatures near the coast did allow some of the snow to change to sleet, freezing rain, and rain for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent light snow along a weak warm front developed on the evening of the 29th over parts of southwestern Maine. Secondary low pressure developed in the Gulf of Maine overnight and snowfall intensified through the early morning hours on the 30th. Snow quickly ended from west to east in the morning, with occasional mixed precipitation. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred at times during the afternoon of the 9th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 13 inches. Gusty winds in the wake of the storm produced areas of blowing snow across northern areas during the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the region during the 1st. Snow developed through the early morning hours of the 1st then persisted through early afternoon. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred through the morning. Warning criteria snow accumulations also occurred through the morning. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to around 12 inches across northwest Aroostook county. Gusty winds produced areas of blowing snow across northern areas during the afternoon. South of the track...across Downeast areas...precipitation transitioned to a wintry mix and rain. Winds gusted to 40 to 50 mph along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low which brought snow to eastern Maine during the 3rd continued to intensify while tracking across the Gulf of Saint Lawrence to Labrador during the 4th. The tight pressure gradient between the low and high pressure to the southwest supported sustained west to northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 50 mph. The winds combined with recent snow to produce extensive blowing and drifting snow across portions of Aroostook county. The greatest impacts occurred on roads bordered by open fields and exposed to the winds. The blowing snow drifted across roads and reduced visibility creating very hazardous driving conditions. The drifting snow also partially melted and re-froze leaving a thin glaze of ice on roads leading to slippery roads. Several accidents were attributed to the hazardous conditions. A jail transport van and another van were involved in a head-on collision on Route 1 in Bridgewater resulting in several injuries. An ambulance responding to the initial incident was then also involved in an accident with another vehicle. The accidents led to the closure of Route 1 for several hours during the morning. Several other rural roads were closed at various times through the day. Later that evening...strong cross winds near Monticello toppled an empty tractor-trailer on Route 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained west to northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 50 mph...created extensive blowing and drifting snow along with slippery road conditions. The hazardous travel conditions led to accidents and road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing in the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to portions of northwest Maine. Snow developed during the afternoon of the 8th and persisted into the early morning hours of the 9th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the early morning hours of the 9th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River in the vicinity of Washburn led to flooding which resulted in the closure of several roads. The ice jam was due to the combination of a pre-existing ice jam from earlier in the winter and a new ice jam. Rising river levels in the vicinity of the ice jams led to the eventual closure of several roads. Closures were reported on Parsons Road...Castle Hill Road...South Wade Road and Gardner Creek Road. The ice jam released during the late evening hours of the 21st. The surge of water with the ice jam release forced chunks of ice onto portions of Route 164 near Crouseville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River near Washburn led to flooding and the eventual closure of several roads. Closures were reported on Parsons Road...Castle Hill Road...South Wade Road and Gardner Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rain events along with the melting of a deep snow pack led to flooding of rivers and streams across Aroostook county. Small river and stream flooding began to develop during the afternoon of the 15th. Flooding developed in Bridgewater along the Dead Brook which eventually led to the closure of Packard Road and occasionally impacted Route 1 into the morning of the 16th. Additional flooding developed during the morning of the 16th with water occasionally covering portions of Russell Road near Fort Fairfield due to elevated river levels and an ice jam. Route 2 was closed in Island Falls due to flooding along Dyer Brook which kept the road closed for several days. The Saint John River at Fort Kent reached flood stage during the evening of the 22nd and crested around a foot above flood level during the morning of the 23rd. The flooding in Fort Kent mostly affected park land bordering the river. The Fish River at Fort Kent crested slightly under flood stage during the evening of the 23rd. The river approached the bottom of the Main Street Bridge...with mostly lowland flooding along the river. Heavy rains occurred from the 19th into the 20th with totals generally ranging from around an inch to in excess of 2 inches. This rain and continued snow melt worsened the small river and stream flooding from the 20th into the 22nd. During this time many small rivers and streams reached bankfull or overflowed leading to numerous reports of lowland and poor drainage flooding across the region along with road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow melt led to flooding of rivers and streams across Aroostook county from Fort Kent south to Island Falls. Lowland and poor drainage flooding occurred in many areas along with numerous road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow melt along with discharge from Grand Lake Matagamon Dam led to flooding along the East Branch of the Penobscot River downriver from the dam to Grindstone. The flooding led to the eventual closure of Route 11 in Grindstone at the railroad bridge and Hay Brook crossing. Lowland flooding also occurred along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow melt along with discharge from Grand Lake Matagamon Dam led to flooding along the East Branch of the Penobscot River. The flooding led to the eventual closure of Route 11 in Grindstone at the railroad bridge and Hay Brook Crossing. Lowland flooding also occurred along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow melt led to flooding along the Mattawamkeag River. The river reached flood stage during the morning of the 17th...then crested slightly over 2 feet above flood stage from the 23rd into the 24th. Bancroft Road was closed due to flooding during the morning of the 22nd and remained closed for several days. Lowland flooding also occurred along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow melt led to flooding along the Mattawamkeag River. The flooding led to the eventual closure of Bancroft Road during the morning of the 22nd...with the road remaining closed for several days. Lowland flooding also occurred along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional rain and snow melt along with continued discharges from Grand Lake Matagamon Dam led to another round of flooding along the East Branch of the Penobscot River. The flooding led to a second closure of Route 11 at the railroad bridge and Hay Brook crossing in Grindstone...the same location where closures occurred several days earlier. A second round of lowland flooding also occurred along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional rain and snow melt along with continued discharges from Grand Lake Matagamon Dam led to another round of flooding along the East Branch of the Penobscot River. The flooding led to another closure of Route 11 in Grindstone at the railroad bridge and Hay Brook Crossing...the same location where closures occurred several days earlier. Lowland flooding also again occurred along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trof and cold pool aloft with an upper low crossed the region during the afternoon of the 10th. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon. Some of the thunderstorms became severe...mostly producing large hail due to the cold pool aloft. Most of the severe thunderstorms occurred across Aroostook county...with an isolated severe thunderstorm also occurring in coastal Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were toppled near Toddy Pond by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. Dime size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine toward Nova Scotia during the 3rd. Snow developed during the morning of the 3rd and persisted into the afternoon. Snow fell at the rate of 1 to 2 inches per hour in a narrow band which extended into northern Washington and southeast Aroostook counties. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the early afternoon hours of the 3rd. Storm total snow accumulations across northern Washington county ranged from 7 to 10 inches. A sharp gradient of snow existed across southeast Aroostook county...ranging from 2 to 4 inches across northwest areas to 6 to 10 inches across eastern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 12th surface low pressure formed over the southern Plains, lifting north and strengthening into the Great Lakes. By the evening of the 12th low pressure had deepened to around 995 mb. A warm front extended to the east of low pressure, held in place by a 1038 mb high pressure centered over Quebec. The strong low pressure in the Great Lakes and the exit region of a greater than 100 knot jet streak over the Canadian Maritimes was supporting strong and broad warm air advection over the Northeast. With cold and dry air in place precipitation initially began as snow across northern New England. Secondary low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic coast and strengthened south of Long Island, ensuring that low level cold air remained in place. Aloft warmer air surged northward, eventually changing precipitation to sleet and freezing rain over much of New Hampshire. Early on the morning of the 13th dry air aloft brought a quick end to precipitation from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the late afternoon and quickly became moderate to heavy in the evening. By late evening warmer air aloft changed precipitation to a mix of snow and sleet. The mix continued into the early morning hours when precipitation quickly ended from west to east. Combined snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 21st a an upper trough was swinging through the Great Lakes, becoming negatively tilted over the Northeast. Surface low pressure formed off the Mid Atlantic in response and quickly began to deepen at all levels. Snow developed ahead of the low pressure late on the 21st and continued into the 23rd. Low pressure eventually tracked into central Maine, which introduced some warmer air and mixed precipitation towards the end of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on the 21st but did not become heavy at times until early on the 22nd. With a warmer late season boundary layer a combination of dynamic, evaporational, and upslope cooling allowed precipitation to stay mostly snow in the mountains of western Maine. Lower elevations mixed with rain and sleet at times. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches in the higher elevations, with amounts closer to 3 to 5 inches in valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of rain on the 26th and 27th of April caused area rivers to rise slightly above bank full resulting in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1 to 3 inches resulted in minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs), which crested at 44,008 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of rain on the 26th and 27th of April caused area rivers to rise slightly above bank full resulting in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1 to 3 inches resulted in minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta (flood stage 12.0 ft), which crested at 13.49 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of rain on the 26th and 27th of April caused area rivers to rise slightly above bank full resulting in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1 to 3 inches resulted on minor flooding on Presumpscot River in Westbrook (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 8.35 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southern stream shortwave tracked along the Gulf Coast on the 3rd with low pressure forming along a stationary front draped across the area. Meanwhile a strong northern stream shortwave was crossing the Great Lakes and southern Canada. As the two waves began to phase off the Mid Atlantic low pressure began to rapidly deepen. Snow spread far to the north of the low center as mid level warm air advection increased on the evening of the 3rd. Snow continued into the morning hours of the 4th as a heavy band of snow developed from central parts of southern New England into coastal New Hampshire and western Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began on the evening of the 3rd. As low pressure intensified off Cape Cod an intense band of snow developed northwest of the track. Though the heaviest snow remained just off shore, parts of this band clipped coastal western Maine with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour early in the morning on the 4th. Snow quickly ended by mid morning. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal thunderstorm complex developed west of the region during the early morning hours of the 21st. The complex then intensified to severe levels while tracking from the Central Highlands to eastern portions of Interior Downeast Maine through the morning. The complex produced large hail and damaging winds along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled on to power lines by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional rainfall and continued snow melt led to flooding of small rivers and lakes across northern Aroostook county from the evening of the 24th into the morning of the 28th. Portage Lake began to reach flood levels during the evening of the 24th. Lake flooding then persisted into the early morning hours of the 28th. The flooding impacted mostly camps along the lake with water eventually reaching 5 to 8 structures. The flooding also impacted roads bordering the lake. The Fish River reached flood stage during the late evening of the 27h and crested less than half a foot above flood stage during the evening of the 28th. The Soldier Pond Bridge was closed during the morning of the 28th...with additional lowland flooding. The Fish River then fell below flood stage during the morning of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow melt led to flooding from the Fish River to Portage Lake. Flooding on the Fish River led to the closure of the Soldier Pond Bridge. Flooding around Portage Lake impacted camps and roads bordering the lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across Downeast areas late on the 24th...exiting across the Maritimes during the 25th. Snow expanded north across the region through the late morning and early afternoon of the 24th. Snow...heavy at times...then persisted across northern areas into the early morning hours of the 25th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late evening of the 24th. Storm total snow accumulations across northern Aroostook county generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches. Across northern portions of Somerset...Piscataquis and Penobscot counties accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Across central portions of the forecast area the snow transitioned to a wintry mix through the evening of the 24th...with the wintry mix transitioning to mostly rain Downeast which led to lesser snow accumulations across those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along the New England coast during the night of the 12th...tracking across Downeast areas during the 13th. Snow expanded north across the region from the late evening of the 12th through the early morning hours of the 13th with increasing intensity. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred during the morning of the 13th. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow across northern areas. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the morning of the 13th. Storm total snow accumulations north of a line from southern Piscataquis to northern Washington counties generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...locally up to around 18 inches. Snow accumulations across Downeast areas generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches along with around 1/4 inch of sleet and some freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...locally up to 12 inches...along with some sleet and freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low which brought heavy snow to northern Maine during the 24th and 25th continued to intensify while tracking toward Labrador and slowing during the 26th. The pressure gradient between the intense low and high pressure to the west produced strong gusty winds during the 26th. West to northwest winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph with frequent gusts of 35 to 45 mph and occasional gusts in excess of 50 mph. The winds combined with the recent heavy snow to produce extensive blowing and drifting snow across portions of Aroostook and northern Penobscot counties during the 26th. The worst conditions occurred along roads bordered by open fields. Visibilities were frequently reduced to near zero with whiteout conditions. Drifting snow partially or totally blocked some roads. The limited visibilities and drifted roads led to very hazardous travel conditions. Portions of both Routes 1 and 11 were closed along with numerous other rural roads. Schools...businesses and State Offices were closed across much of Aroostook county. In excess of 20 weather related accidents were reported across Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds and recent snow combined to produce extensive blowing and drifting snow. Significantly reduced visibilities with whiteouts and drifted roads led to very hazardous travel conditions along with road closures and accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 12th surface low pressure formed over the southern Plains, lifting north and strengthening into the Great Lakes. By the evening of the 12th low pressure had deepened to around 995 mb. A warm front extended to the east of low pressure, held in place by a 1038 mb high pressure centered over Quebec. The strong low pressure in the Great Lakes and the exit region of a greater than 100 knot jet streak over the Canadian Maritimes was supporting strong and broad warm air advection over the Northeast. With cold and dry air in place precipitation initially began as snow across northern New England. Secondary low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic coast and strengthened south of Long Island, ensuring that low level cold air remained in place. Aloft warmer air surged northward, eventually changing precipitation to sleet and freezing rain over much of New Hampshire. Early on the morning of the 13th dry air aloft brought a quick end to precipitation from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the evening and quickly became moderate to heavy in the late evening. After midnight warmer air aloft allowed some of the snow to mix with sleet at times. The snow continued into the morning hours when precipitation quickly ended from west to east. Snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

By the evening of the 12th low pressure had deepened to around 995 mb. A warm front extended to the east of low pressure, held in place by a 1038 mb high pressure centered over Quebec. The strong low pressure in the Great Lakes and the exit region of a greater than 100 knot jet streak over the Canadian Maritimes was supporting strong and broad warm air advection over the Northeast. With cold and dry air in place precipitation initially began as snow across northern New England. Secondary low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic coast and strengthened south of Long Island, ensuring that low level cold air remained in place. Aloft warmer air surged northward, eventually changing precipitation to sleet and freezing rain over much of New Hampshire. Early on the morning of the 13th dry air aloft brought a quick end to precipitation from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the evening and quickly became moderate to heavy in the late evening. After midnight warmer air aloft allowed some of the precipitation to mix with sleet at times. The snow and sleet continued into the morning hours when precipitation quickly ended from west to east. Snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along the New England coast during the night of the 12th...tracking across Downeast areas during the 13th. Snow expanded north across the region from the late evening of the 12th through the early morning hours of the 13th with increasing intensity. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred during the morning of the 13th. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow across northern areas. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the morning of the 13th. Storm total snow accumulations north of a line from southern Piscataquis to northern Washington counties generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...locally up to around 18 inches. Snow accumulations across Downeast areas generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches along with around 1/4 inch of sleet and some freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 17 inches. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph produced considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across Downeast areas late on the 24th...exiting across the Maritimes during the 25th. Snow expanded north across the region through the late morning and early afternoon of the 24th. Snow...heavy at times...then persisted across northern areas into the early morning hours of the 25th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late evening of the 24th. Storm total snow accumulations across northern Aroostook county generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches. Across northern portions of Somerset...Piscataquis and Penobscot counties accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Across central portions of the forecast area the snow transitioned to a wintry mix through the evening of the 24th...with the wintry mix transitioning to mostly rain Downeast which led to lesser snow accumulations across those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of October 15th a sharp upper trough dropped through the Upper Midwest, with surface low pressure moving into the Great Lakes. As the primary low pressure began to occlude on the 16th secondary low pressure development occurred on the Mid Atlantic coast. When the upper trough became negatively tilted the secondary low pressure rapidly deepened and tracked through New England overnight into the morning of the 17th. Low pressure ultimately bottomed out at 972 mb. Prior to the secondary low pressure occluding a strong low level jet lifted northeast across the coast of New England with strong wind gusts overnight on the 16th into the 17th.  Uprooted and snapped trees along with downed limbs caused significant power outages along coastal Maine.  Total power outages for the event in the state peaked around 200,000 customers without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts in the early morning hours of the 17th led to downed trees and power lines across coastal York County. Local mesonet weather stations measured wind gusts ranging between 45 and 55 mph. Across Maine nearly 200,000 power outages were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported at a grocery store.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed by straight line thunderstorm winds near South Jefferson. nickel size hail reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across northwest Maine to the Gulf of Saint Lawrence during the 1st...drawing a cold front across the region producing damaging winds. The high winds across northern and eastern Maine occurred in several phases. In the first phase...onshore southerly winds with a very strong low level jet affected Downeast areas during the morning. The most significant impacts with this phase generally occurred along the Downeast coast. Wind gusts along the coast generally ranged from 55 to 65 mph...with isolated gusts up to around 70 mph. The second phase occurred with winds associated with a convective line of showers along the cold front which also crossed the region during the morning. Wind gusts with the line of showers generally ranged from 40 to 50 mph. These winds affected much of the region. The third phase of the event occurred with west-southwest gradient winds which developed during the afternoon in the wake of the cold front and persisted into the late afternoon and evening. Wind gusts with this phase of the event generally ranged from 50 to 60 mph...with the greater impacts across northern and central portions of the region.|Power outages rapidly increased during the 1st...peaking at around 62000 customers across northern and eastern Maine. Power was not completely restored in some areas until later on the 3rd. Numerous trees were snapped or toppled along with broken branches. Utility poles were also toppled or damaged in many areas. The fallen trees and utility poles blocked many roads...leading to road closures and slowing the recovery process. Heavy rains occurred with the system leading to wet soils. The wet soil and changing wind directions likely contributed to the large number of toppled trees. Damage to the roof and siding of an elementary school was reported in Surry...along the Hancock county coast. A stained glass window was blown out of a church in Caribou...in northeast Aroostook county.

A thunderstorm develop during the evening hours near Yarmouth, Maine. This storm had a lot of instability to work with and produced a short-lasting microburst in Yarmouth. A storm also produced with damage in Kittery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst downed several trees in and around Yarmouth. Power was out for most of the town.

Weakening low pressure approached from the west during the 27th. Secondary low pressure then intensified across the Gulf of Maine through the night of the 27th into Thanksgiving morning the 28th. The intensifying low exited across the Maritimes during the 28th. Snow developed during the evening of the 27th and persisted through Thanksgiving morning. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the morning of the 28th. A corridor of heavy snow occurred in a region from northern Somerset...to southeast Aroostook...to northern Washington...to southern Piscataquis counties. Storm total snow accumulations in this region generally ranged from 7 to 11 inches...with isolated totals up to around 12 inches. Lesser snowfall occurred north of this corridor. Warmer temperatures...along with a wintry mix...limited snow accumulations across Downeast areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

Intensifying low pressure tracked across northwest Maine to the Gulf of Saint Lawrence during the 1st...drawing a cold front across the region producing damaging winds. The high winds across northern and eastern Maine occurred in several phases. In the first phase...onshore southerly winds with a very strong low level jet affected Downeast areas during the morning. The most significant impacts with this phase generally occurred along the Downeast coast. Wind gusts along the coast generally ranged from 55 to 65 mph...with isolated gusts up to around 70 mph. The second phase occurred with winds associated with a convective line of showers along the cold front which also crossed the region during the morning. Wind gusts with the line of showers generally ranged from 40 to 50 mph. These winds affected much of the region. The third phase of the event occurred with west-southwest gradient winds which developed during the afternoon in the wake of the cold front and persisted into the late afternoon and evening. Wind gusts with this phase of the event generally ranged from 50 to 60 mph...with the greater impacts across northern and central portions of the region.|Power outages rapidly increased during the 1st...peaking at around 62000 customers across northern and eastern Maine. Power was not completely restored in some areas until later on the 3rd. Numerous trees were snapped or toppled along with broken branches. Utility poles were also toppled or damaged in many areas. The fallen trees and utility poles blocked many roads...leading to road closures and slowing the recovery process. Heavy rains occurred with the system leading to wet soils. The wet soil and changing wind directions likely contributed to the large number of toppled trees. Damage to the roof and siding of an elementary school was reported in Surry...along the Hancock county coast. A stained glass window was blown out of a church in Caribou...in northeast Aroostook county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail. A few small trees were downed as well.

Weakening low pressure approached from the west during the 27th. Secondary low pressure then intensified across the Gulf of Maine through the night of the 27th into Thanksgiving morning the 28th. The intensifying low exited across the Maritimes during the 28th. Snow developed during the evening of the 27th and persisted through Thanksgiving morning. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the morning of the 28th. A corridor of heavy snow occurred in a region from northern Somerset...to southeast Aroostook...to northern Washington...to southern Piscataquis counties. Storm total snow accumulations in this region generally ranged from 7 to 11 inches...with isolated totals up to around 12 inches. Lesser snowfall occurred north of this corridor. Warmer temperatures...along with a wintry mix...limited snow accumulations across Downeast areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked northeast across Downeast Maine through the night of the 11th into the 12th...exiting across the Maritimes late on the 12th. The low produced heavy snow across much of northern and northwest Maine. Snow developed through the afternoon and evening of the 11th. Snow...heavy at times...then persisted through early on the 12th before ending during the late afternoon. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the morning of the 12th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 11 inches...locally up to around 12 inches across the Saint John Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened explosively while tracking from the Mid-Atlantic region toward southern Maine through the overnight hours of the 16th into the morning of the 17th. A strong low level jet crossed the region through the morning of the 17th in advance of the low. Along the Downeast coast...easterly winds were sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts of 60 to 70 mph. Across interior Downeast areas winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 50 to 60 mph.|The winds snapped or toppled numerous trees along with branches. The falling trees and snapped branches brought down power lines and utility poles. Power outages increased rapidly through the early morning hours of the 17th. Power outages peaked during the morning of the 17th at around 45000 customers across northern and eastern Maine. The most extensive power outages occurred across Hancock county...where nearly half of the power outages were reported. Downed tress blocking roads and the extent of damage slowed the recovery process. Power was not entirely restored across coastal portions of Hancock county until the 20th. The Deer Isle area was particularly impacted. Trees were reported snapped or toppled across much of Downeast Maine. However...damage was also concentrated around Acadia National Park where the falling trees prompted many road closures. Another area of concentrated damage was along Route 1...and side roads...in coastal Washington county where numerous reports of downed trees and power lines led to road closures.

Isolated thunderstorms developed near the Midcoast of Maine on the afternoon of the 24th, at least one of which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail piled up several inches in a few spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed near the Midcoast of Maine on the afternoon of the 24th, at least one of which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell near Rockland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed near the Midcoast of Maine on the afternoon of the 24th, at least one of which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail reported in the northern portion of North Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing coastal low moved up the Northeast coast from Long Island, New York across Cape Cod, Massachusetts and into the Gulf of Maine on October 28th.  This allowed for northeasterly winds up to 25 kts to generate nearshore waves of 6 to 8 feet and a storm surge of 1 to 1.5 feet.  This modest storm coincided with a period of high astronomical tides, with the King Tide being the primary reason for the coastal flood event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A King Tide combined with a weak coastal low which brought a foot of storm surge and near shore waves of 6 to 8 feet during the noon high tide.  Storm tide water levels peaked at 12.45 feet at the Portland tide gauge.  Flood stage is 12 ft.  Minor flooding occurred on local wharf side streets near Js Oyster Bar in Portland with up to 1 foot of inundation.  Splash-over was also reported at numerous locations along coastal roads due to pounding surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved developed near the Midcoast of Maine on the afternoon of July 2nd and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees downed in Rockport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the region in advance of an approaching cold front. Showers and thunderstorms began developing late morning...then persisted through the afternoon into the evening. Some of the thunderstorms became severe during the afternoon...producing large hail and damaging winds.

A cold front moved into New England from the west on the 6th of July. The air mass ahead of the front became moderately unstable and aided in the development of thunderstorms, a few of which were severe across central and southern Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down on Paris and Oxford roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed on River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot temperatures occurred across the region during the 20th. Across portions of the Central Highlands and Downeast Maine apparent temperatures reached Heat Advisory levels. The combination of temperatures ranging from the upper 80s to the mid 90s...with dewpoints around 70...produced Heat Index values ranging from the mid 90s to near 100 degrees during the afternoon. A cold front crossed the region during the 21st with lower temperatures and dewpoints in the wake of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of temperatures ranging from the upper 80s to mid 90s...with dewpoints around 70...produced Heat Index values ranging from the mid 90s to near 100 degrees during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal thunderstorm complex developed west of the region during the early morning hours of the 21st. The complex then intensified to severe levels while tracking from the Central Highlands to eastern portions of Interior Downeast Maine through the morning. The complex produced large hail and damaging winds along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were toppled around Brownville by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the region in advance of a cold front during the 31st. Thunderstorms initially developed late morning across northern areas...then through the afternoon across the remainder of the region. An isolated severe thunderstorm occurred across Northeast Aroostook county late morning...with another severe thunderstorm across southern Piscataquis county late afternoon. Both thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled in the vicinity of Washburn by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front working in tandem with moderate instability aided in the development of scattered thunderstorms across the region. A few became severe and produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed on Legion Park Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf and strong rip currents contributed to a drowning death of a man on August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Surf with short period breaking waves of 4 to 5 feet and attendant strong undertow aided in a drowning death at Long Sands Beach in York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trof crossed the region during the afternoon of the 12th within a high shear environment. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon with the trof. One of the thunderstorms produced a brief tornado touchdown in northern Washington county. The touchdown occurred in a wooded area near Route 6. The EF-1 tornado intermittently touched down along a quarter mile path with winds estimated at up to 90 mph. An estimated 120 trees were snapped or toppled along the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mini-supercell thunderstorm produced a brief tornado in a wooded area of Kossuth Township between Baskahegan Stream and Route 6. The tornado briefly touched down twice along the short path along Iron Bridge Road. Wind speeds with the EF-1 tornado were estimated at up to 90 mph. Approximately 120 trees were uprooted or snapped along the path (DI 28, DOD4). The average path width was around 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed near the Maine and New Hampshire border in the late afternoon hours of August 3rd in advance of a cold front. The atmosphere was moderately unstable with high temperatures well into the 80s. One thunderstorm produced damage in Lebanon, Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power poles down in the vicinity of Lower Guinea Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into southernmost Maine during the afternoon hours of the 7th. The air mass was very moist in advance of a slow-moving cold front with dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to around 70F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles snapped with wires down on William Oscar Emery Drive in Sanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into southernmost Maine during the afternoon hours of the 7th. The air mass was very moist in advance of a slow-moving cold front with dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to around 70F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down on state Route 109 near Mousam Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down near Shapleigh Corner Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front aided in the development of a few thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 13th. One storm became severe over York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed on wires near Blackberry Hill Road in Berwick.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down near Route 219.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine through the morning hours of the 3rd...exiting across the Maritimes through the afternoon and evening. A wintry mix developed across Downeast areas during the late evening of the 2nd. Precipitation...mostly in the form of snow...then expanded north across the remainder of the region through the morning of the 3rd. Snow...heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour...persisted across northern areas into the early evening. Snow then diminished through the late evening hours. A wintry mix of snow...sleet and freezing rain persisted across Downeast areas through the morning then diminished during the afternoon. Warning criteria sleet accumulations occurred across Downeast areas through the morning of the 3rd. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late morning into the afternoon across northern areas. Storm total snow accumulations from eastern Aroostook across northern and central Penobscot counties generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches. Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches occurred in area from central and southern Piscataquis county into northwest portions of southern Penobscot county. Storm total sleet accumulations across much of Downeast Maine ranged from 1.00 to 2.00 inches along with up to around a quarter of an inch of ice and 1 to 3 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total sleet accumulations ranged from a half inch up to 2 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of snow and up to around a quarter inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine through the morning hours of the 3rd...exiting across the Maritimes through the afternoon and evening. A wintry mix developed across Downeast areas during the late evening of the 2nd. Precipitation...mostly in the form of snow...then expanded north across the remainder of the region through the morning of the 3rd. Snow...heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour...persisted across northern areas into the early evening. Snow then diminished through the late evening hours. A wintry mix of snow...sleet and freezing rain persisted across Downeast areas through the morning then diminished during the afternoon. Warning criteria sleet accumulations occurred across Downeast areas through the morning of the 3rd. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late morning into the afternoon across northern areas. Storm total snow accumulations from eastern Aroostook across northern and central Penobscot counties generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches. Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches occurred in area from central and southern Piscataquis county into northwest portions of southern Penobscot county. Storm total sleet accumulations across much of Downeast Maine ranged from 1.00 to 2.00 inches along with up to around a quarter of an inch of ice and 1 to 3 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting snow combined with an inch of rainfall causing already swollen rivers in Maine to rise above flood stage. Flooding was generally minor but some rivers remained above flood stage for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting snow combined with an inch of rain resulting in minor  flooding on the Androscoggin River at Rumford (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 16.0 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across Downeast Maine through the 31st...exiting across the Maritimes that night. Snow developed across Downeast areas during the early morning hours of the 31st then expanded northward across the rest of the region through the remainder of the morning. Snow...heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour...persisted across portions of Northeast and East-Central Maine through the afternoon. Snow then diminished through the evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon hours. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches in a zone from Mars Hill...to Millinocket...to Houlton. A sharp gradient of lesser snow totals occurred north and west of this zone toward the Saint John Valley and North Woods. Warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to a wintry mix across interior Downeast areas and to rain along the Downeast coast. This limited snow accumulations in those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

Branches were reported snapped...with shingles torn from some buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm briefly turned severe downing trees in southern Somerset County around the town of Canaan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Canaan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Dorian tracked south of the Gulf of Maine during the 7th...making landfall near Halifax Nova Scotia that afternoon. Sustained winds across central and coastal portions of Washington county generally ranged from 20 to 30 mph...with peak gusts of 40 to 45 mph. Trees were reported down along Route 192 in Northfield and along Route 191 in Baring Plantation. Lesser winds occurred across coastal portions of Hancock county where peak wind gusts ranged from 30 to 40 mph. Several trees were toppled along Seawall Road. A large branch was snapped and fell on a vehicle...damaging the vehicle...in Southwest Harbor. The heaviest rains fell across eastern Washington county generally ranging from 2.00 to 3.00 inches...with isolated totals in excess of 3.00 inches. Rainfall diminished to the west...with maximum totals across Hancock county generally between 1.00 and 1.50 inches. With the exception of minor ponding...hydrologic problems were not an issue during the storm. Neither coastal flooding or storm surge were a problem during the event. The lowest barometric pressure reported was 994.7 MB at the Jonesport Buoy. The maximum sustained winds at Eastport were 36 mph and 30 mph at West Quoddy. The maximum wind gust reported over land with this event was 46 mph at Eastport. Over water...the maximum wind gust reported was 52 mph at Mount Desert Rock at an elevated platform.

Low pressure deepened explosively while tracking from the Mid-Atlantic region toward southern Maine through the overnight hours of the 16th into the morning of the 17th. A strong low level jet crossed the region through the morning of the 17th in advance of the low. Along the Downeast coast...easterly winds were sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts of 60 to 70 mph. Across interior Downeast areas winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 50 to 60 mph.|The winds snapped or toppled numerous trees along with branches. The falling trees and snapped branches brought down power lines and utility poles. Power outages increased rapidly through the early morning hours of the 17th. Power outages peaked during the morning of the 17th at around 45000 customers across northern and eastern Maine. The most extensive power outages occurred across Hancock county...where nearly half of the power outages were reported. Downed tress blocking roads and the extent of damage slowed the recovery process. Power was not entirely restored across coastal portions of Hancock county until the 20th. The Deer Isle area was particularly impacted. Trees were reported snapped or toppled across much of Downeast Maine. However...damage was also concentrated around Acadia National Park where the falling trees prompted many road closures. Another area of concentrated damage was along Route 1...and side roads...in coastal Washington county where numerous reports of downed trees and power lines led to road closures.

A closed upper low progressed across the northern half of the U.S. with multi-center surface lows pinwheeling around it.  One surface low approached New England on the evening of December 1st. A band of heavy snow moved into extreme southwestern Maine before stalling and weakening early on the 2nd. Snow with occasional mixed precipitation  continued off and on through the 2nd across extreme southwestern Maine with surface low pressure stalled near the 40N/70W benchmark. On the 3rd a strong shortwave trough approached from the northwest and surface low pressure once again deepened and retrograded into the Gulf of Maine. This led to the development of several bands of heavy snow on the 3rd that rotated westward across western Maine through the day. Eventually these weakened and moved east, bringing snow to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little snowfall occurred December 1st through December 2nd, with precipitation remaining too far south. The majority of the accumulation came on the 3rd as surface low pressure retrograded into the Gulf of Maine. Several bands of heavy snow developed and rotated westward over western Maine before weakening. Snowfall rates in the heavy bands exceeded 1 inch per hour at times. Around midday the dry slot moved into parts of the Midcoast and snow lightened up and mixed with sleet or freezing rain at times. This kept storm total snowfall on the lower side, generally ranging from 4 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Great Lakes early on November 27th. Ahead of the storm a warm front lifted into New England. Low and mid level temperatures remained relatively warm for the duration of the storm for much of the area, but at onset there was light snow and mixed precipitation. Secondary low pressure developed overnight in the Gulf of Maine and helped to draw colder air into the system. This kept the precipitation type in the western Maine mountains predominately wet, heavy snow. Snow continued into the morning of the 28th before coming to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as wet snow in the western Maine mountains and remained mostly snow as secondary low pressure developed in the Gulf of Maine. Snow became heavy at times during the evening of the 27th, with numerous reports of thunder snow from the White Mountains through Carrabassett Valley. The heaviest snowfall came to an end early on the 28th, but light snow lingered into the afternoon. Total snowfall ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure meandered into the Ohio River Valley on the evening of the November 23rd and began to weaken as secondary development took place over coastal North Carolina. This secondary area of low pressure then rapidly strengthened on the 24th and spread rain and snow over northern New England. Late on the evening of the 24th an upstream shortwave kicked the storm out to sea, limiting the overall duration of snow in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By midday on the 24th precipitation overspread the western Maine mountains. Strong lift helped cool a marginal air mass so that any rain or mix flipped to moderate to heavy snow in the afternoon and evening. This was especially true where easterly flow was upsloping into the higher terrain. Total snow accumulations were generally in the 6 to 8 inch range on the east side of the terrain, and 4 to 6 inches on the western side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 29th a primary low pressure moved into the Upper Midwest with a warm front draped off to the east. Air rising over the warm front brought snow into extreme western Maine after midnight on the 30th. Dry air from the northeast kept the moderate to heavy band of snow more or less stationary through the day.  As the upper trough approached in the afternoon snow finally began to move to the northeast. A secondary area of low pressure developed off the southern New England coast around midnight on the 31st and spread more widespread snow across western Maine. Generally light snow fell on the 31st with dry air from the east leading to less snow as you got farther from the New Hampshire border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation moved in very early on December 30th as snow at onset. Early on the morning of the 30th continued warm air advection aloft allowed a nearly stationary band of moderate to heavy snow to develop overhead and remain in place into the afternoon. Secondary low pressure developed off the southern New England coast after midnight on the 31st and prolonged light snow across the area into the morning. Snow totals ranged from 4 inches near Mooselookmeguntic Lake to 12 inches in the Mahoosuc Mountains.

As the upper trough approached in the afternoon snow finally began to move to the northeast. A secondary area of low pressure developed off the southern New England coast around midnight on the 31st and spread more widespread snow across western Maine. Generally light snow fell on the 31st with dry air from the east leading to less snow as you got farther from the New Hampshire border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation moved in late on December 29th as snow at onset. Early on the morning of the 30th continued warm air advection aloft allowed a nearly stationary band of moderate to heavy snow to develop overhead and remain in place into the afternoon. Secondary low pressure developed off the southern New England coast after midnight on the 31st and prolonged light snow across the area into the morning. Snow totals ranged from 9 to 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system (999 mb) crossed through western New England during a period of high astronomical tides on December 14th. This allowed for gale force southeasterly winds along the coast at high tide, leading to nearshore waves of 12 feet every 11 seconds along coastal York County at high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The astronomical tide in Portland of 10.6 feet combined with a storm surge of 1.9 feet at high tide to reach a 12.5 foot storm tide during the midday hours.  Flood stage in Portland is 12.0 feet (MLLW).  The tide remained above flood stage for 90 minutes at the Portland tide gage.  Nearshore waves peaked around 12 feet with an 11 second periodicity, which caused significant erosion of the Marginal Way Trail in Ogunquit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast of the U.S. produce 3 to 5 inches of rain in a short period of time on mostly frozen ground, resulting in flash flooding in southwest Maine. Many streams and creeks flooded due to the heavy rain with the hardest hit areas being across coastal York and Cumberland counties.  Many roads were closed across this area.  In Biddeford a sinkhole formed causing damage to the sewer system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streams and creeks flooded due to the heavy rain.  Many roads were closed in much of York county.  In Biddeford a sinkhole formed causing damage to the sewer system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast of the U.S. produce 3 to 5 inches of rain across southwest Maine. This resulted in minor flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Presumpscot River at Westbrook crested at 18.43 ft which is 3.43 ft above flood stage.  This lead to minor flooding on the river.  There was some damage to equipment at a construction project on the river in Westbrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed near the Midcoast of Maine on the afternoon of the 24th, at least one of which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail on the northeastern portion of North Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low progressed across the northern half of the U.S. with multi-center surface lows pinwheeling around it.  One surface low approached New England on the evening of December 1st. A band of heavy snow moved into extreme southwestern Maine before stalling and weakening early on the 2nd. Snow with occasional mixed precipitation  continued off and on through the 2nd across extreme southwestern Maine with surface low pressure stalled near the 40N/70W benchmark. On the 3rd a strong shortwave trough approached from the northwest and surface low pressure once again deepened and retrograded into the Gulf of Maine. This led to the development of several bands of heavy snow on the 3rd that rotated westward across western Maine through the day. Eventually these weakened and moved east, bringing snow to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of December 1st the heavy snow band failed to reach Cumberland County. Relatively little accumulation occurred through December 2nd, with occasional snow showers or mixed precipitation. The majority of the accumulation came on the 3rd as surface low pressure retrograded into the Gulf of Maine. Several bands of heavy snow developed and rotated westward over western Maine before weakening. Snowfall rates during the mid morning hours exceeded 1 inch per hour at times. Storm total snowfall generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches.
